preFix.title: Macramé window valance
preFix.subtitle: A 60 cm wide × 20 cm deep flat macramé valance in 3 mm cotton cord, mounted on a wooden dowel
preFix.excerpt: A flat panel of alternating square knots and diagonal double half-hitch lines, mounted on a 65 cm wooden dowel. Natural white 3 mm single-twist cotton cord. Finished panel: 60 cm wide × 20 cm deep before fringe, trimmed straight at 5 cm.
preFix.sourceNotes: Flat macramé valance construction is standard practice in contemporary macramé instruction. Format, knot sequence, and cord calculations are original. Not drawn from a specific published source.

content.text: This valance is a flat macramé panel designed to hang across the top of a window, softening the frame without blocking light. It uses 3 mm single-twist cotton macramé cord in natural white, mounted on a 65 cm wooden dowel. The finished woven area measures 60 cm wide by 20 cm deep, with a 5 cm straight-trimmed fringe below the last row. The pattern alternates rows of
lines that run across the full width, giving the panel a structured geometric character. All cords are mounted with
. The knot count is moderate and the layout is regular, making this a good project after the basics of

items.name: 3 mm single-twist cotton macramé cord, natural white — 24 cords, each cut to 1.8 m (giving 0.9 m working length when folded)
Wooden dowel, 65 cm long, 10 to 12 mm diameter — for mounting and hanging
Macramé board or clipboard — to pin work to while knotting
Macramé T-pins — for holding cords in position on the board
Macramé fringe comb or stiff-bristled brush — for brushing the fringe

content.text: Cut 24 lengths of 3 mm single-twist cotton macramé cord, each measuring 1.8 m. When each cord is folded in half and mounted on the dowel with a lark's head knot, it contributes two working tails of 0.9 m. With 24 cords mounted, you will have 48 working tails hanging below the dowel. This is sufficient cord for 20 cm of knotted panel plus the 5 cm fringe. Lay all cords out and count before starting.

Suspend the dowel horizontally at a comfortable working height. Fix it to a macramé board with T-pins at each end, or hang it from a hook at eye level. Fold the first cord in half and place the folded loop over the dowel from the front. Pull both tails through the loop to form a lark's head knot. Repeat with all 24 cords, spacing them evenly so the knots sit flush against each other. The 48 tails should span the 60 cm working width with knots roughly 1.25 cm apart. Pin the outer tails to the board edge to keep the panel from shifting.
The panel follows a repeating sequence of three rows. Refer to the square knot and double half-hitch technique entries to confirm knot construction before starting.

definition.caption: Alternating square knots: each row offsets by two cords to build an open diamond lattice

content.text: Group the 48 tails into sets of 4. The outer two tails of each group are the working cords; the inner two are the

. Tie one square knot per group across the full width: 12 knots total. Work at a consistent tension, pulling each knot snug but not distorting the cord. Leave approximately 1 cm of clear cord between the dowel and the top of each knot.
Row 2: Diagonal double half-hitch line (left to right)

Take the leftmost tail (cord 1) and hold it diagonally across all remaining tails at roughly 45 degrees, pointing toward the lower right. This becomes the carrier cord. Using each of the remaining 47 tails in sequence from left to right, tie one double half-hitch around the carrier cord. Pull each hitch firmly so the knots sit snug against each other on the carrier. The diagonal line will travel from the upper left to the lower right across the full panel width.

Leave the first 2 tails free at the left edge. Working in groups of 4 from cord 3 to cord 46, tie one square knot per group: 11 knots total. Leave the last 2 tails free at the right edge. This offset row creates the alternating diamond structure. The free edge tails will be incorporated in the next full row.

Return to Row 1 (full-width alternating square knots) and continue the three-row sequence: full square knots, diagonal double half-hitch line, offset square knots. Repeat until the knotted panel reaches 20 cm below the dowel. Measure from the underside of the mounting knots, not from the dowel itself. Four or five complete repeats of the three-row sequence will reach the intended depth depending on cord tension.

Once the knotted panel reaches 20 cm, lay the work flat and identify the last row of square knots. Measure 5 cm below the lowest knot and mark with a T-pin across the width. Cut all tails straight at this mark, using sharp scissors. Comb the fringe straight with the macramé fringe comb, working downward from the knot line in short strokes. Natural single-twist cotton will separate into individual plies when combed, giving a fluffy, full fringe; leave it as-is or trim back to 5 cm for a neater edge. To hang the valance, loop a length of cord or thin wire through each end of the dowel and mount on two wall hooks.

items.fix: Pin the far end of the carrier cord to the board at the intended exit point before starting the hitch row. Keep tension on the carrier throughout; let it up only to re-pin.
items.cause: The carrier cord is not held taut at a fixed angle while hitching
items.problem: The diagonal double half-hitch line curves rather than running at a consistent angle

items.fix: Measure the width at every third row and re-pin the outer tails to the board edge. Work each square knot by pulling the working cords snug to the filler cords, not to the previous knot.
items.cause: Inconsistent knot tension: over-tight knots draw the working cords inward; loose knots let them spread
items.problem: The panel narrows or widens as it progresses

items.fix: Lay a ruler or strip of card at the 5 cm mark and cut along it. Trim in small sections rather than cutting all tails in one pass.
items.cause: Tails were cut freehand rather than against a straight guide
items.problem: The fringe looks uneven after trimming

items.fix: Check that all cords were cut to exactly 1.8 m before mounting. If the dowel bows under the cord weight, add a third hook at the centre top.
items.cause: Uneven cord lengths or a bowed dowel
items.problem: The panel droops or won't hang flat

initialFindings.message: Tutorial PUBLISHED with heroMediaId IS NULL — hero-fill must be run
PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
